coredata.dc:description: This study presents a room temperature optical fiber ammonia (NH3) sensor based on a polyacrylamide/metal organic framework-carbon quantum dots(–COOH) (PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH)) gas-sensitive film, whose performance was further enhanced with ultraviolet (UV) light. The gas-sensitive film is elaborated by the dipping-pulling method, applying PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH) to the surface of a cascade-spliced fiber structure based on single mode fiber (SMF), multimode fiber (MMF), hollow core fiber (HCF), MMF and SMF (S-MHM-S). Experimental results show that the sensor achieves a sensitivity of 17 pm/ppm at room temperature, representing a 3.4-fold increase compared to the scenario without UV light enhancement. The sensor has a limit of detection (LOD) of 1 ppm, with response and recovery times of 3.97 s and 3.52 s, respectively. The response mechanism of MOF-CQDs(–COOH) to NH3 is described in detail with the results of density functional theory (DFT) calculation. The proposed fiber NH3 sensor operates at room temperature and has great potential in practical application.

(a) Preparation process of the PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH) gas-sensitive film and schematic diagram of the synthesis process of PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH) gas-sensitive film by including (b) PAM gel, (c) Zn + and PAM composite gel, and (d) PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH) gel.
Optimized crystal plane model of (a) MOF-74, (b) CQDs(–COOH), (c) MOF-74(1 0–1) and (d) MOF-CQDs (–COOH).
Gas sensing system of the optical fiber sensor.

The surface material distribution of the optical fiber sensor includes its (a) micrograph, and corresponding SEM images of (b) cross-section, (c) enlarged cross-section and (d) surface material, (e) TEM of MOF-CQDs(–COOH), (f) local amplification of TEM, (g) TEM of CQDs(–COOH) clusters and (h) TEM of CQDs(–COOH), (i) EDS of MOF-CQDs(–COOH) (10 repeated experiments) and elemental mapping image of (j) MOF-CQDs(–COOH), (k) Zn, (l) O, (m) C.

Characterization of MOF-CQDs(–COOH) including (a) XRD, (b) FTIR and (c) fluorescence spectrum, XPS curves of MOF-CQDs(–COOH) material, including (d) overall spectrum, (e) zinc spectrum, (f) oxygen spectrum and (g) carbon spectrum, (h) spectrogram and (i) spatial spectrogram of optical fiber sensor, (j) spectral response curve and (k) optical power-wavelength response curve of the sensor in 100 ppm NH3 under different UV light power (10 repeated experiments).

Spectral sensitivities of the optical fiber sensor (a) without UV assistance and (b) with UV assistance, (c) time response curve of the optical fiber sensor, NH3 sensitivity curves (d), (e) without UV assistance (10 repeated experiments), (f) sensitivity curve comparison (10 repeated experiments), NH3 sensitivity curves (g), (h) with UV assistance (10 repeated experiments), (i) sensitivity curve comparison (10 repeated experiments).
Adsorption model and ELF of NH3 at (a), (d) MOF-74 surface, (b), (e) near MOF surface of MOF-CQDs(–COOH) and (c), (f) near CQDs(–COOH) surface of MOF-CQDs(–COOH).

FMO distribution of different end faces (a) NH3, (b) MOF-74 and (c) MOF-CQDs(–COOH), (d) adsorption energy under different adsorption models, (e) refractive index curves and (f) light absorption spectra of different adsorption models.
Schematic diagram of gas-sensitive mechanism of MOF-CQDs(–COOH) in (a) air, (b) NH3 and (c) NH3 environment under UV photosensitization.

Mass spectrometry of the reaction products after passing through NH3, the mass of the response molecular labels, including (a)N2, (b) NO, (c) N2O and (d) NO2 mass spectrometry results (M28: N2; M30: NO; M44: N2O; M46: NO2), (e) photocurrent response of PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH) gas-sensitive film.

(a) Gas dynamic response and (b) sensitivity curve (10 repeated experiments) of the optical fiber NH3 sensor, (c) selectivity curve, (d) response curve in different gas mixtures, (e) long-term stability (10 repeated experiments), (f) cyclic test stability, (g) humidity response gradient curve and (h) humidity sensitivity curve (10 repeated experiments) of the optical fiber NH3 sensor.
Comparison results of frontier electron orbital distribution.
Comparison results between this work and other reports on ammonia gas sensing.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents a room temperature optical fiber ammonia (NH3) sensor based on a polyacrylamide/metal organic framework-carbon quantum dots(–COOH) (PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH)) gas-sensitive film, whose performance was further enhanced with ultraviolet (UV) light. The gas-sensitive film is elaborated by the dipping-pulling method, applying PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH) to the surface of a cascade-spliced fiber structure based on single mode fiber (SMF), multimode fiber (MMF), hollow core fiber (HCF), MMF and SMF (S-MHM-S). Experimental results show that the sensor achieves a sensitivity of 17 pm/ppm at room temperature, representing a 3.4-fold increase compared to the scenario without UV light enhancement. The sensor has a limit of detection (LOD) of 1 ppm, with response and recovery times of 3.97 s and 3.52 s, respectively. The response mechanism of MOF-CQDs(–COOH) to NH3 is described in detail with the results of density functional theory (DFT) calculation. The proposed fiber NH3 sensor operates at room temperature and has great potential in practical application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ammonia (NH3) is frequently encountered in various fields such as agriculture, industry, and daily life [1–4], where hazards have been posed to the environment and human health [5,6]. Studies have shown that NH3 concentrations as low as 50 ppm can be harmful to the respiratory system and skin [7], while exposure to 500 ppm may result in permanent damage [8]. Therefore, the development of NH3 sensors is particularly crucial for optimizing its performance with high sensitivity, limit of detection (LOD) and the ability to operate at room temperature.

In recent years, the high surface-area-to-volume ratio, porous nature, and structural diversity of metal organic frameworks (MOFs) and their derivatives have demonstrated significant potential for gas sensing. For instance, Cu-MOF [9], MOF-based ZnO/ZnFe2O4 (ZZFO) [10], and ZIF-67/PAN [11] have been reported for the fabrication of high-performance semiconductor resistance sensors. Quantum dots (QDs) offer the advantage of a high surface area as potential candidate for gas-sensitive materials. Particle sizes comparable to the Debye length suggest the rapid internal electron transfer [12], becoming an excellent merit for improving the LOD and response time of gas sensors. SnO2 colloidal QDs provide the abundant active sites for gas adsorption, exhibiting extremely high selectivity for H2S gas [13]. Gas sensor based on In2O3 colloidal QDs shows a fast response to H2S gas at room temperature [14].

Composite materials combining MOFs with carbon QDs (CQDs) leverage the porous structure of MOFs and the outstanding optical properties of CQDs [15]. Their excellent electron transfer properties have been attributed to the abundant crown ether groups on the surface of CQDs [16]. When these functional groups interact with other substances, they can enhance electron mobility and induce changes in energy levels. Hydrogen bonding between metal ions and the carboxyl or hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of CQDs facilitates the transfer of photo-generated electrons to coordinated heavy metal ions, thereby enabling their detection [17].

This study incorporates a composite material consisting of MOF-74 encapsulated CQDs, and being fixed within polyacrylamide (PAM). PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH) gas-sensitive film was prepared and elaborated onto the surface of optical fibers using a pull-coating method for detecting NH3 at room temperature. Relying on the optical properties of CQDs, the sensitivity is enhanced under ultraviolet (UV) light assistance. The optical fiber structure based on single mode fiber (SMF), multimode fiber (MMF), hollow core fiber (HCF), multimode fiber, and single mode fiber (S-MHM-S) effectively launches and confines the evanescent field on the fiber surface, enabling the capture of refractive index changes occurring in the gas-sensitive film exposed to NH3 at room temperature.

All reagents used in this work were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology. Co. and used without further purification. Ultra-pure water was used in the whole experiment. The reagents and their purities used in the experiment are as follows: ethylenediamine (C2H8N2, 99.0 % purity), PAM (98.5 % purity), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn (NO3)2·6H2O, 99.0 % purity), ammonium persulfate (APS, 98.5 % purity), N, N, N, N-tetramethylethylenediamine (TEMED, 99.0 % purity), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.8 % purity), triethylamine (TEA, 99.0 % purity), 2,5-dihydroxyterephthalic acid (C8H6O6, 98 % purity). The corn stalks used in the experiment were collected in Shenyang, Liaoning Province, China.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and simulation of optical fiber structure
ce:section.ce:para: This study employs the S-MHM-S cascaded structure for effective manipulating the evanescent field in the region of HCF. The dipping-pulling method is used to coat the surface of the S-MHM-S structure with a PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH) gas-sensitive film, sensitizing the optical fiber structure based NH3 sensor.

The preparation process of the S-MHM-S structure is as follows: SMF is fused with a cut of MMF with the length of 300 μm, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). In Fig. 1(b), HCF with an inner diameter of 19 μm and an outer diameter of 140 μm is 35 μm offset-fused with MMF. Fig. 1(c) and Fig. 1(d) show the schematic and microscope image of S-MHM-S structure, respectively. The MMF in the S-MHM-S structure is used to expand the mode field area launched into the cladding wall of the HCF, thereby enhancing the intensity of the evanescent field on the surface of the HCF.

The S-MHM-S structure enables the evanescent field to be excited on the surface of the HCF. In this study, the mode evolution of the optical field within the S-MHM-S structure was simulated and analyzed. As shown in Fig. 1(e), the simulation model of the S-MHM-S structure is presented, while Fig. 1(f) illustrates the simulation results, demonstrating that light entering from the offset position continues to propagate along the inner wall of the HCF. As light transmits from SMF into the MMF, there is a significant expansion of the mode field (shown in Fig. 1(g)). A portion of light leaks into the outer wall of the HCF, forming an evanescent field on its surface, as demonstrated in Fig. 1(h). The evanescent field in the S-MHM-S structure primarily concentrates on the outer wall of the HCF (shown in Fig. 1(i)). The evanescent field on the surface of HCF can be fully utilized to enhance the penetration depth of light on the surface of the gas sensitive film. Consequently, the S-MHM-S structure serves as an excellent carrier for gas-sensitive films, enabling gas sensing measurements at the micro scale.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH) gas sensitive films
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation process of the PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH) gas-sensitive film is illustrated in the Fig. 2 (a). 10 g of corn straw powder was soaked in a 5 % C2H8N2 solution by mass percentage. Under continuous reaction conditions, the mixture was hydrothermally reacted at 160 °C for 16 h. After the reaction, the CQDs(–COOH) were obtained and purified for 72 h.

PAM/MOF gels were prepared using in-situ synthesis techniques [18]. PAM was dissolved in 50 ml of deionized water to prepare a 1 % mass fraction solution. Subsequently, 0.17 g of Zn (NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved in the solution. APS was added to the solution to achieve a mass percentage of 0.65 %, and TEMED was added with a mass percentage of 1.55 % (Fig. 2(b)). After stirring, DMF, TEA, and C8H6O6 were added to the mixture with mass percentages of 0.24 %, 0.55 %, and 0.82 %, respectively (Fig. 2(c)). Then, 500 μL of the prepared CQDs(–COOH) solution was added, and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 24 h to obtain the PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH) solution (Fig. 2(d)).

The film was coated three cycles using a dipping-pulling machine with specific parameters: a descent speed of 250 μm/s, a dwell time of 10 s, and an ascent speed of 500 μm/s. Then, the fiber was air-dried for 2 h to solidify the PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH) gas-sensitive film on the fiber structure.

The density functional theory (DFT) was used to explore the adsorption behavior and surface reaction mechanism of gas [19]. CASTEP is used to optimize the model and DMol3 is used to calculate the parameters. CCDC: 1,999,760 was used for the Zn-MOF-74 optimized initial geometry, as shown in Fig. 3 (a). The CQDs(–COOH) model is shown in Fig. 3(b), which is an aromatic system containing –COOH and –OH functional groups [20]. The MOF-74(1 0–1) crystal plane (Fig. 3(c)) was selected as the NH3 adsorption platform in this study. Fig. 3(d) shows the lowest energy system of MOF-CQDs(–COOH), based on which the principle of NH3 adsorption is studied. The exchange–correlation energy is calculated using Perdew, Burke and Emzerhof (PBE) of Generalized Gradient Estimation (GGA). The Energy cutoff is 400 eV and the k-point grid is 2x2x1. The adsorption energy can be calculated by Eq. (1):
(1)

where, Es is the energy of the original surface, and Egas is the energy of the gas, Es + gas refers to the surface energy after absorbing gas.

Fig. 4 shows the gas detection system of optical fiber evanescent field NH3 sensor with PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH) gas-sensitive films. The system primarily consists of an amplified spontaneous emission (ASE, spectral range: 1528 nm-1603 nm), an optical spectrum analyzer (OSA, resolution: 0.02 nm) and a gas cylinder providing the gas (O2:N2 = 1:4). A gas chamber equipped with a UV light source features a 365 nm light-emitting diode (LED) light source (Shenzhen, Yingfeng Optoelectronics) that provides adjustable power densities of 5 mW/cm2, 9 mW/cm2, 14 mW/cm2, 17 mW/cm2, 23 mW/cm2, and 27 mW/cm2. Air flow is controlled by mass flow meter (MFM, Seven Star CS200). Meanwhile, the mass flow controller ensures a constant airflow into the chamber, preventing pressure fluctuations caused by airflow variations. The humidity adjustment system of the system is shown in the blue dashed box in Fig. 4 to adjust the humidity level in the chamber. The dynamic detection system is used to verify the dynamic detection performance of the sensor for NH3 (red dotted box in Fig. 4). This system includes a 1550 nm distributed-feedback (DFB) laser, a photodetector (PD) and an oscilloscope. It is utilized to analyze the dynamic characteristics of the sensor, such as time response and repeatability. The response of sensor was measured by a photoelectric detection system, and its dynamic response to gas was evaluated in volts. This testing method was previously reported in our earlier study [21]. Specifically, the system uses a laser with a wavelength of 1550 nm as the light source. When the spectrum of optical fiber sensor undergoes a redshift, the light intensity at 1550 nm changes, modulating the intensity of the laser passing through the sensor. The PD then converts this light signal into an electrical signal, which is recorded by an oscilloscope. Through this demodulation mechanism, as the gas concentration around the sensor increases, the spectral shift continuously alters the laser power within the sensor. The PD translates these changes in optical power into variations in the voltage signal, outputting the response of sensor in volts.

The gas used for testing is extracted from a saturated solution. The gas prepared by this method has high reliability and has been reported in many studies [22–24]. Moreover, since the injected gas volume was minimal, it did not cause a significant pressure change within the chamber, making the impact of pressure variation on the test results negligible. These liquids include ammonia solution (ACS, ≥28 %) acetone (ACS, ≥99 %), benzene (ACS, ≥99.7 %), butanone (ACS, ≥99.5 %), ethyl ethanol (ACS, ≥99.7 %), methanol (ACS, ≥99 %). The saturated vapor pressure of a liquid can be determined by Antoine equation shown in Eq. (2).
(2)
lg P = A - B / ( T + C )
where P is the vapor pressure of the substance, T is the temperature. A, B and C are searchable constants. According to the ideal gas law, after knowing the saturated vapor pressure of the liquid, the gas volume required for a specific concentration of gas can be determined by Eq. (3) [25].
(3)
C =
P t

where Pt is the saturated vapor pressure of the target gas, which can be determined by Antoine equation. Vt is the volume of the target gas, Pm is the saturated vapor pressure of the mixture, Vm is its volume, and C is the concentration of the gas. Because of the high volatility of ammonia solution, its saturated vapor pressure is difficult to determine. Therefore, NH3 gas is prepared by volatilization of ammonia solution. The volume of ammonia solution required for a given concentration of NH3 gas can be determined by Eq. (4) [26].
(4)

where ρ is the density of the desired liquid (g/ ml), w is the purity of the liquid, M is the molecular weight of the liquid (g/mol), V1 is the volume of the desired liquid (L), and V2 is the volume of the gas chamber (L). Finally, a specific concentration of ammonia was obtained by heating a certain volume of ammonia solution in the gas chamber. Before each gas injection, dry air is injected into the gas chamber through MFM1 to ensure the same test environment each time.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and steady-state/transient fluorescence spectroscopy were employed to investigate the elemental composition and microscopic morphology of the composites. Specifically, SEM (Zeiss G500, Germany) was used to examine the surface topography of the material, while TEM (Tecnai G2 F20) analyzed the distribution of CQDs on its surface. XRD (Shimadzu, Japan) was utilized to determine the composition of the composite material using the CuKα radiation source. XPS (Thermo Scientific, America) characterized the elemental states within the material with the AlKα radiation source, and FTIR (Thermo Fisher IS5, America) identified functional groups present in the material within the spectral range of 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1. The steady-state/transient fluorescence spectroscopy (Edinburgh FLS1000, Britain) provided insights into the optical properties of the material in the spectral range of 300 nm to 800 nm.

Fig. 5 (a) presents a microscopic image of the sensing area, demonstrating the uniform distribution of the material on the optical fiber surface without aggregation. Fig. 5(b) displays an SEM cross-section of the fiber, providing insight into the thickness of the gas-sensitive film. As shown in the figure, the thickness of the gas-sensitive film is 1.4 μm. Fig. 5(c) illustrates the distribution of gas-sensitive materials on the fiber surface. The image reveals that the gas-sensitive layer is uniformly distributed, confirming its successful deposition on the fiber. Fig. 5(d) shows the surface morphology of the film, which is uniform and free of aggregation. This is attributed to the formation of the MOF-CQDs(–COOH) material on the PAM molecular chains. The ordered molecular structure of PAM prevents the aggregation of the MOF-CQDs(–COOH) material. Such dispersion is particularly important for gas sensing, as it increases the contact surface area between the gas molecules and the material, effectively accelerating gas adsorption and desorption, thereby enhancing the sensitivity and response time of the sensor.

Fig. 5(e) shows the TEM images of the Zn-MOF-74 material encapsulating CQDs(–COOH). It is evident that the material has an average diameter in the nanometer range and exhibits a rod-like morphology. Fig. 5(f) refers to the internal structure of CQDs(–COOH) clusters encapsulated within the MOF becomes clearly visible. Fig. 5(g) reveals details of CQDs(–COOH) clusters with a diameter of 42 nm. These QDs are effectively aggregated within the framework during MOF growth, successfully achieving the encapsulation of QDs. Fig. 5(h) provides a detailed view of individual QDs, revealing a diameter of approximately 5.6 nm for CQDs(–COOH). A closer examination of the TEM images further confirms the presence of CQDs(–COOH) within the Zn-MOF-74 material. Fig. 5(i) represents the energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) spectrum of the material, displaying strong peaks for Zn, O, and C elements, further confirming the elemental composition of the material.

Fig. 5(j) presents the material mapping image of MOF-CQDs(–COOH). Fig. 5(k), (l), and (m) respectively depict the mapping images of Zn, O, and C elements. The MOF presents an ordered lattice structure and CQDs(–COOH) are embedded in the MOF structure in a highly dispersed manner. This ordered interlocking not only showcases the synergistic effect between Zn-MOF formation and CQDs, but also highlights the successful integration of CQDs(–COOH) into the Zn-MOF structure. It is noteworthy that the mapping images clearly reveal the uniform distribution of CQDs(–COOH) within the Zn-MOF, confirming their successful integration into the Zn-MOF structure.

The XRD pattern (Fig. 6 (a)) reveals the crystal structure of the MOF-CQDs(–COOH) composite. The diffraction peaks at 26.36°, 33.18°, and 54.64° match those of JCPDS card no. 211486, indicating that the zinc element in the composite exists in the form of an oxide. The diffraction peaks at 11.44°, 22.24°, and 26.36° correspond to JCPDS card no. 440558, which is primarily attributed to the presence of CQDs(–COOH) in the composite, demonstrating the widespread distribution of carbon in the material [27]. Fig. 6(b) shows the FTIR of MOF-CQDs(–COOH). Absorption properties of 3288.36 cm−1 are related to the stretching frequencies of –OH and –OH on carboxyl groups. The characteristic peak at 1733.21 cm−1 clearly indicates the presence of the CO functional group in the material, further confirming the carboxylation of CQDs [28]. The prepared CQDs(–COOH) solution could not fluoresce due to aggregation. The fluorescence spectrum of the CQDs(–COOH) solution with a mass fraction of 0.5 %wt is shown in the Fig. 6(c). The excitation wavelength was 453 nm, indicating that CQDs(–COOH) was successful prepared.

In-situ XPS analysis helps to reveal the changes in the functional groups of the MOF-CQDs(–COOH) gas-sensitive layer before and after UV irradiation, providing deeper insight into the gas-sensing mechanism of sensor. Fig. 6(d) shows the XPS survey spectrum of MOF-CQDs(–COOH), where the peaks correspond to the Zn, O, and C elements. In Fig. 6(e), the prominent peaks of Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2 have binding energies of 1044.4 eV and 1022.4 eV, respectively. This indicates that zinc exists in its oxide form, confirming the formation of Zn-MOF. UV irradiation affects the electronic structure of Zn 2p, with the characteristic peaks of Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2 shifting towards higher binding energies by 0.25 eV and 0.1 eV, respectively. This change may be due to the enhanced interaction between the photogenerated electrons from CQDs(–COOH) and Zn2+ under UV light, leading to a decrease in the electron cloud density of Zn, which increases its effective binding energy [29]. Fig. 6(f) shows the in-situ XPS spectra of O 1 s before and after UV irradiation. The lower binding energy peak at 531.6 eV is related to oxygen in Zn-MOF, while the higher binding energy peak is associated with the carbonyl group (–COOH) in CQDs. This confirms the presence of the carboxyl functional group in CQDs(–COOH). After UV irradiation, the oxygen characteristic peak related to the carbonyl group (–COOH) shifts towards a higher binding energy by 0.5 eV, indicating that UV light affects the electronic structure of the carbonyl group, potentially leading to the separation of photogenerated carriers in CQDs(–COOH) under light, thus reducing the electron density of the carbonyl group [30]. This also provides more reactive oxygen species on the surface of the gas-sensitive layer. Fig. 6(g) shows the in-situ XPS spectra of C 1 s, where the peaks for CC are at 284.8 eV, COC at 286.3 eV, and O-CO at 288.6 eV, all originating from the carboxyl group in CQDs(–COOH). The peak at 290.7 eV may be related to carbonates formed from the hydrothermal reaction of straw. UV irradiation causes the characteristic peak of COC to shift towards a lower binding energy by 0.15 eV, while the characteristic peak for OC=O shifts towards a lower binding energy by 1.1 eV. This significant shift suggests that UV light may trigger the dissociation or reconstruction of the carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs(–COOH), resulting in significant changes in their electron density and chemical environment. This could be related to the redistribution of photogenerated electrons and holes, affecting the surface chemistry of CQDs and thereby enhancing the gas-sensing response of the sensor.

Fig. 6(h) illustrates the spectra of the S-MHM-S structure (blue curve), the S-MHM-S structure with PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH) gas-sensitive film (black curve), and the structure under UV excitation (red curve). After coating the surface with the PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH) gas-sensitive film, the contrast value of the sensor spectrum decreases, while the interference pattern remains unchanged. This occurs because some of the transmitted light entering the film experiences an intensity loss due to the refractive index difference, but the propagation path remains unaffected, preserving the interference pattern of the spectrum. Under UV irradiation, the refractive index of PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH) gas-sensitive films changes, resulting in a blue shift in the sensor spectrum. Fig. 6(i) shows the spatial spectrogram. The spatial light pattern increases after UV excitation. This is because the change of refractive index of the film exerts the impact on the transmitted light path and forms a new light mode.

The effect of UV power density on sensor performance was investigated through an optimization experiment. As shown in Fig. 6(j), under a 100 ppm NH3 environment, variations in UV power density result in different degrees of spectral shifts in the sensor response, indicating a significant impact of light intensity on sensor characteristics. Fig. 6(k) shows that a moderate increase in UV power density enhances the sensing signal. When the UV power density reaches 17 mW/cm2, the spectral shift reaches its maximum, suggesting that the number of active sites in the gas-sensitive layer is maximized, leading to the strongest ammonia adsorption capacity. However, when the power density exceeds a certain threshold, the spectral shift tends to saturate or even decline. This may be due to the saturation of electronic transitions in the gas-sensitive layer at excessively high UV intensities, where further increasing the light intensity does not significantly enhance gas adsorption. Additionally, excessive UV irradiation may accelerate the recombination of photogenerated carriers, reducing the photo-sensitization effect and ultimately affecting the response performance of sensor. Therefore, in this study, a UV power density of 17 mW/cm2 was selected to ensure optimal gas-sensing performance of the optical fiber sensor.

At room temperature (25 ℃), NH3 gas with a concentration of 0 ∼ 120 ppm was detected. The gas sensitivity study of optical fiber sensor was carried out under two conditions: no UV light and sensitivity enhanced under UV lighting. The gradient interval is 20 ppm. The results are shown in Fig. 7 (a) and (b). The spectrum gradually redshifts with the increase of NH3 concentration due to the presence of NH3, where a change undergoes in the refractive index of PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH) gas-sensitive film on the fiber surface. Specifically, changes in the refractive index of the gas-sensitive film influence the propagation path of light within the film. When light within the sensitive film returns to the fiber core, the interference spectrum of the core light is altered. This change is dependent on the gas concentration, enabling the sensor to measure the concentration of NH3 in the environment. Fig. 7(c) shows the repeatability and time response characteristics of the sensor to 20 ppm NH3. The sensor demonstrates good repeatability, with a response time of 3.97 s and a recovery time of 3.52 s, indicating a fast response that is significant for practical applications.

Compares the gas sensitivity of the optical fiber sensor under conditions with and without UV light enhancement at the maximum and minimum wavelength shifts of the interference peaks. In the absence of UV irradiation, the sensor has the smallest NH3 sensitivity of 5 pm/ppm at 1552 nm and the largest sensitivity of 5 pm/ppm at 1592 nm (as shown in Fig. 7(d), (e)). This is because the adsorbed oxygen on the surface of the gas-sensitive film combines with NH3, changes the thickness of the MOF-CQDS(–COOH) depletion layer, and then causes the change of the refractive index of the sensitive film, so the signal of the sensor is modulated. Fig. 7(f) compares the minimum sensitivity of the sensor under UV irradiation and without UV irradiation, indicating that the UV auxiliary sensor has a higher gas sensitivity, which is ∼ 3 times than that of the non-UV auxiliary sensor. Under UV light, the sensor has a minimum gas sensitivity of 16 pm/ppm at 1546 nm and a maximum sensitivity of 17 pm/ppm at 1589 nm (as shown in Fig. 7(g), (h)). The small difference between the maximum and minimum sensitivity indicates the overall superior performance of the gas-sensitive film, avoiding localized sensitivity issues.

Similarly, a comparison of the maximum sensitivities (Fig. 7(i)) shows that the gas sensitivity of the UV-assisted sensor is ∼ 3.4 times higher than that of the non-UV assisted sensor. These results show that the optical fiber gas sensor based on PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH) gas-sensitive film has high sensitivity under UV light assistance. This is because UV light can excite a significant number of photogenerated electrons and holes in MOF-CQDs(–COOH), which increases the number of adsorption sites for NH3 on the surface of material, thereby enhancing the sensitivity of sensor.

DFT shows the specific details of NH3 molecule adsorption on the material surface. Fig. 8 (a) shows the adsorption model of NH3 on MOF-74 surface, Fig. 8(b) shows the adsorption model of NH3 near MOF surface of MOF-CQDs(–COOH), and Fig. 8(c) shows the adsorption model on CQDs(–COOH) surface. Electron localization function (ELF) has important application value in studying the properties of chemical bonds and lone pair electron pairing, and is used to describe electron localization [31]. ELF values of 1.851 × 10−34, 5.672 × 10−2 and 1.134 × 10−1 represent extremely low charge density, completely delocalized electrons and completely localized electrons, respectively [32], which well describe the electron domestication and delocalization between gas molecules and gas sensitive layers. The extremely low frequency emission of NH3 molecules on MOF-74 monolayer in Fig. 8(d) shows that the electrons in the interlayer region are completely delocalized. Therefore, there is a strong interaction between NH3 and MOF-74 surface, but this interaction does not lead to the formation of chemical bonds [33]. Similarly, when delocalization electrons still exist between CQDs(–COOH) and MOF-74 layer in Fig. 8(e), it indicates that there is no chemical bond between NH3 and gas sensing layer. In Fig. 8(d), there is a more pronounced electron delocalization between NH3 and the CQDs(–COOH) layer, indicating a stronger interaction between NH3 and CQDs(–COOH). Similarly, this strong interaction does not result in the formation of chemical bonds. As shown in Fig. 8(d)-(f), although electronic delocalization occurs between the gas molecules and the sensitive layers, it does not lead to the formation of new chemical bonds within the system. On the contrary, this interaction is dominated by vdW, which is the main reason why NH3 can be desorbed [34].

Combined with the results of DFT calculation, the distribution of frontier molecular orbital (FMO) of MOF-74 was studied after the addition of CQDs(–COOH). Fig. 9 (a) shows the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) distributions of NH3. Prior to the addition of CQDs(–COOH), the HOMO and LUMO of MOF-74 were mainly distributed on its surface and internally (Fig. 9(b)). However, upon the addition of CQDs(–COOH), the molecular orbitals underwent a rearrangement, with the HOMO and LUMO of MOF-74 shifting towards the level of CQDs(–COOH) (Fig. 9(c)) [35,36]. Additionally, CQDs(–COOH) exhibited an enrichment of electron clouds on its surface, enhancing its ability to adsorb NH3, consistent with the analysis of ELF.

Additionally, the energy gap between the LUMO and HOMO levels of MOF-CQDs(–COOH) is only 0.000711 eV (Table 1 ), which is smaller than that of MOF-74. This implies that electron transitions are easier, thereby enhancing the gas adsorption capability of material. The gap between LUMO and HOMO levels of NH3 varies with different surfaces. Compared to MOF-74, the energy gap between NH3 and MOF-CQDs(–COOH) is smaller, measuring only 0.200785 eV. This makes NH3 more prone to adsorption on the surface of MOF-CQDs(–COOH) [37–39].

Fig. 9(d) shows the adsorption energy of different adsorption models. When NH3 is adsorbed on the surface of CQDs(–COOH), the adsorption energy of the system reaches the maximum and the system is the most stable (−29.0125 eV), which is consistent with EFL analysis. This is mainly because the addition of CQDs(–COOH) changes the charge distribution on the surface of MOF and increases the gas adsorption capacity of MOF-CQDs(–COOH). Adsorption often leads to changes in the optical properties of materials. Based on DFT calculations, refractive index change curves for different systems were obtained (Fig. 9(e)). When NH3 is adsorbed onto the surface of MOF-CQDs(–COOH), a significant change in the refractive index of material occurs in the infrared region. This change triggers the optical signal output of the optical fiber sensor, facilitating the detection of NH3. Fig. 9(f) illustrates the spectral absorption curves of the materials under different systems. Similarly, in the infrared region, there is a variation in the light absorption of material [40]. This alteration directly results in the absorption of evanescent waves by the material at the optical fiber surface, consequently affecting the output signal at the fiber end.

In PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH) gas sensing film, MOF-CQDs(–COOH) material plays a leading role in NH3 detection. The MOF material enhances gas adsorption sites and enriches NH3 within it, boosting local gas concentration [41]. In addition, the carboxyl group of MOF-CQDs(–COOH) can form hydrogen bond with NH3, which increases the adsorption sites of NH3 [42].

In addition, the interaction between NH3 and MOF-CQDs (–COOH) is mainly explained by the adsorption–desorption model [43,44]. CQDs(–COOH) are p-type semiconductor materials and the internal carriers are mainly holes [45]. Oxygen on the surface of CQDs(–COOH) materials traps electrons in their conduction bands and forms adsorptive O2(ads)
− (Eqs. (5) and (6)), which leads to the increase of hole concentration in CQDs(–COOH) and the formation of hole accumulation layer (HAL) on the surface of CQDs(–COOH) (Fig. 10 (a)). When materials are exposed to NH3, NH3 reacts with O2(ads)
− in Eqs. (7)-(10) [46–48], and transports a significant number of electrons to CQDs(–COOH) (Fig. 10 (b)). These electrons neutralize with holes on the surface of CQDs(–COOH) and reduce the thickness of HAL, which makes the resistance of the material increase and causes the change of refractive index of the material, thus realizing the detection of NH3 by optical fiber sensor [49,50].

The reaction products on the surface of the gas-sensitive layer were analyzed using mass spectrometry to gain a more detailed understanding of the gas-sensing mechanism. As shown in Fig. 11 (a)-(c), when NH3 is introduced to the surface of the gas-sensitive layer, the levels of N2, NO, and N2O in the products gradually increase. NO shows the highest concentration, while N2O is the lowest. This may be due to NH3 being first oxidized to NO during its reaction with the gas-sensitive layer, and the formation rate of NO is relatively high, leading to its dominance among the products. In contrast, the pathway for N2O formation might be less prominent, resulting in its lower concentration. Fig. 11(d) shows that after NH3 injection, there is almost no formation of NO2, likely due to limited reaction conditions and a shortage of active sites on the gas-sensitive layer. This observation aligns with previously reported studies [51]. The mass spectrometry results further confirm the room temperature gas-sensing mechanism described by Eqs. (8)-(10).

Under UV, a large number of photo-generated electrons (e(photo)
−) and holes (h(photo) + ) are excited in CQDs(–COOH) [52], in which h(photo) + reacts with adsorbed oxygen on the surface of the material to form oxygen and is enriched on the surface of the material (Eq. (11)), while e(photo)
− combine with oxygen to form a new oxygen species O2(photo)(abs)
− (Eq. (12)). According to the results of the above medium mass spectrometry analysis, the new oxygen species O2(photo)(abs)
− can react with the NH3 production by Eqs. (13)-(15). Compared with O2(ads)
−, O2(photo)(abs)
− has higher reactivity at room temperature, and its binding energy with material surface is lower, so it usually has higher reactivity [53]. CQDs(–COOH) excite photogenerated holes and electrons with high separation efficiency, allowing carriers and holes to flow to MOF [54]. The high specific surface area of MOF increases the distribution sites of h(photo) + , which provides more places for NH3 adsorption (Fig. 10(c)). Therefore, UV light sensitizes the ability of sensor to detect NH3 and makes it possible to detect it at room temperature.

As shown in Fig. 11(e), the photocurrent response of the PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH) gas-sensitive film under different UV irradiation conditions is presented. The experimental results indicate that under UV illumination, the photocurrent of the gas-sensitive film increases rapidly and stabilizes during irradiation, suggesting that UV light induces significant photogenerated carrier generation. When the UV light is turned off, the photocurrent decreases rapidly, indicating a fast recombination rate of the photogenerated carriers. A further comparison of photocurrent responses under different UV intensities reveals that higher UV irradiation intensities generate stronger photocurrent signals. This suggests that UV excitation enhances the generation and separation of electron-hole pairs, facilitating charge transport within the gas-sensitive layer. Based on the photocurrent response data, the UV-enhanced sensing mechanism can be inferred as follows: Under UV illumination, photogenerated electrons at the CQDs(–COOH)/MOF interface migrate to CQDs(–COOH), while holes are captured by the MOF conduction band. This process not only effectively reduces carrier recombination but also promotes the photo-desorption of oxygen molecules, increasing the concentration of adsorbed oxygen species [55]. The enhanced presence of these oxygen species improves the adsorption capacity for NH3 molecules, thereby enhancing the sensitivity of sensor.

The evanescent field is generated at the interface between the S-MHM-S structure and the PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH) gas-sensitive film, with a portion of the evanescent light leaking into the interior of the film [18,56]. The evanescent field can be expressed by Eq. (16):

It is confirmed that the penetration depth of the evanescent wave is dependent on the angle of light incidence, the refractive index of the film, and the wavelength of the light. When the sensor is placed in NH3 environment, due to the formation of hydrogen bond between NH3 and MOF-CQDs(–COOH), and the effect of NH3 on the internal electron distribution of MOF-CQDs(–COOH), The refractive index of PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH) gas-sensitive film changes and causes the change of the optical signal output of the sensor. The analysis of these variations allows for the determination of the concentration of NH3 in the environment [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic response characteristics, selectivity and humidity sensing characteristics of gas sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 12 (a) (the black curve) illustrates the dynamic results of the optical fiber NH3 sensor under UV sensitization. The NH3 with the concentration of 1 ppm, 10 ppm, 20 ppm, 30 ppm, 40 ppm, 50 ppm and 60 ppm are injected into the gas chamber successively. The response of sensor increased accordingly. The dynamic response performance of the sensor without UV sensitization was experimentally investigated in Fig. 12(a) (the red curve). Fig. 12(b) compares the sensitivities of the two scenarios during dynamic testing, showing a ∼ 4-fold increase in sensitivity under UV sensitization. These results indicate that UV assistance enhances the responsiveness of sensor to NH3. The UV excitation accelerates the transfer of ground-state electrons to excited-state electrons in CQDs(–COOH) and increases the concentration of excited-state electrons. When NH3 combined with MOF-CQDs(–COOH), more excited-state electrons will convert to the ground-state, causing a faster change in the refractive index of the sensitive film. Fig. 12(a) also illustrates the LOD of the sensor. Under UV sensitization, the sensor can detect NH3 gas at concentrations as low as 1 ppm. In the absence of UV sensitization, the sensor has an extremely low response to 1 ppm of NH3 gas. This indicates that UV sensitization improves the ability of sensor to detect trace amounts of gas to some extent. This is because UV light enhances the sensitivity of the sensor, improving its responsiveness to trace gases. A lower LOD is crucial in the practical application of the sensor, especially in the measurement of trace gases.

Fig. 12(c) depicts the selectivity curve of the sensor. Under UV irradiation, the sensor responds to NH3, methanol, ethanol, acetone, n-butanol, and benzene. However, in the same concentration, the response to NH3 is more than ten times higher than that of other gases, indicating excellent selectivity for NH3 (black curve in Fig. 12(c)). Similarly, without UV assistance, the sensor still exhibits good selectivity for NH3 (red curve in Fig. 12(c)). The mechanism of the excellent selectivity of sensor for NH3 is that the dynamic diameter of NH3 molecules is smaller than that of other gases [58–60], which is conducive to the passage of NH3 molecules through the pores of MOF materials. On the other hand, the larger volume of other gases prevents them from passing through the pores of the MOF and binding with CQDs(–COOH). Therefore, the sensor has good selectivity for NH3.

Cross-interference experiments are essential for comprehensively evaluating the selectivity of the sensor. To further verify its selectivity in complex gas environments, we conducted cross-interference tests in the presence of NH3 and acetone, NH3 and benzene, and NH3 and methanol, with both NH3 and the interfering gases at a concentration of 60 ppm. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 12(d). The results indicate that in the presence of both NH3 and acetone, the sensor still exhibits a strong response to NH3, with only a slight decrease in response due to acetone interference. This effect is likely caused by acetone molecules occupying some active sites on the gas-sensitive layer, reducing NH3 adsorption and leading to a minor decrease in response. However, this interference remains relatively weak, and the sensor can still effectively detect NH3. Moreover, when NH3 coexists with benzene or methanol, the response trend of sensor remains nearly identical to that in a pure NH3 environment, further confirming its excellent selectivity and resistance to interference. These findings demonstrate that while interfering gases may partially occupy the active sites on the gas-sensitive layer, the sensor can still effectively distinguish NH3 and maintain high detection sensitivity.

Long-term stability is a crucial parameter for gas sensors, making its evaluation essential. Fig. 12(e) presents the sensitivity variation of the NH3 sensor. The experimental results indicate that the sensor maintains high sensitivity even after prolonged operation, with minimal sensitivity degradation, demonstrating excellent long-term stability. Fig. 12(f) shows the response variation of the sensor over 20 NH3 adsorption–desorption cycles (NH3 concentration: 60 ppm). The results reveal no significant attenuation in response intensity or recovery performance during the cycles, with a maximum variation of 0.0007 nm. This further confirms the outstanding stability and reliability of sensor.

Fig. 12(g) illustrates the response curves of the sensor to NH3 under humidity conditions of 20 %RH, 30 %RH, 50 %RH, 60 %RH, 80 %RH and 90 %RH. The Fig. 12(h) displays the sensitivity curves to NH3. The sensitivity of sensor is measured at 8.67 × 10−4 V/ppm, 8.55 × 10−4 V/ppm, 8.43 × 10−4 V/ppm, 8.37 × 10−4 V/ppm, 8.33 × 10−4 V/ppm and 8.26 × 10−4 V/ppm, respectively. The decrease in sensitivity with increasing humidity can be attributed to water molecules occupying the surface of the material, thereby reducing the effective contact area for the measured gas molecules. However, the humidity sensitivity of the optical fiber gas sensor is relatively small compared to its sensitivity to NH3. When the humidity changes by 1 %RH, the maximum change in sensor sensitivity is 1 × 10−6 V/ppm, which corresponds to 1 % of the change in sensor NH3 sensitivity. The reason for the low humidity sensitivity of optical fiber NH3 sensor is that MOF-CQDs(–COOH) cross-links the amino groups in PAM, making it unable to form hydrogen bonds with water molecules, resulting in a negligible humidity sensitivity of the gas sensitive film.

Table 2 compares the performance of the optical fiber NH3 sensor proposed in this study with previously reported sensors. The clad-modified optical fiber NH3 sensor based on Cu-functionalized ZnO nanoflakes enables rapid NH3 detection [61]. However, its detection limit is higher compared to the sensor proposed in this study. The graphene-coated in-fiber Mach-Zehnder interferometer (MZI) exhibits high NH3 sensitivity [62], but its long response and recovery times limit its applicability for real-time detection. The Fe2O3 nanotube-coated microfiber interferometer can detect NH3 at room temperature. However, its sensitivity is lower than that of the optical fiber sensor presented in this study [63]. The room-temperature humidity-resistant highly sensitive NH3 sensor based on a porous MXene/Na2Ti3O7@polyaniline composite achieves a detection limit of 1.77 ppm [64], enabling the detection of low-concentration NH3. However, its longer response and recovery times make it less suitable for real-time NH3 monitoring compared to the optical fiber sensor proposed in this study. Similarly, the room-temperature NH3 sensor with high selectivity based on CuO nanosheets and WS2 nanoflakes also suffers from slow response and recovery speeds [65]. The multifunctional porous polyaniline/phosphorus-nitrogen co-doped graphene (PANI/PNGN) nanocomposite-based supercapacitor exhibits a high response and a low detection limit for NH3. However, it has certain limitations in response time. In contrast, the optical fiber gas sensor proposed in this study demonstrates a significantly faster response, making it more advantageous for real-time NH3 detection [66].

A room temperature optical fiber ammonia (NH3) sensor based on polyacrylamide/metal organic framework-carbon quantum dots(–COOH) (PAM/MOF-CQDs(–COOH)) composite material was developed, and its sensitivity was enhanced by ultraviolet (UV) light. Experimental results indicate that UV light sensitization increases the sensitivity of sensor by ∼ 3.4 times. Moreover, the sensor exhibits extremely low humidity sensitivity, measuring only one-thousandth of its sensitivity to NH3, demonstrating superior moisture resistance compared to previously reported NH3 sensors [67,68]. This study proposes a composite strategy in which MOF-74 encapsulates CQDs, with the MOF-CQDs(–COOH) composite material immobilized within a PAM matrix. The oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CQD(–COOH) enhance the electron transport capability of the gas-sensitive layer. Meanwhile, the interaction between CQDs(–COOH) and MOF optimizes the electronic structure of the material, improving its NH3 adsorption capacity. Additionally, this study introduces and verifies a UV regulation strategy, where UV irradiation excites photogenerated carriers, facilitating the formation of adsorbed oxygen species. This enhances the interaction between NH3 and the material surface, thereby improving sensor sensitivity. Compared to previous optical fiber NH3 sensors, the sensor discussed in this study demonstrates higher sensitivity and a lower limit of detection (LOD) [63]. Additionally, by integrating optical fiber sensing technology and optimizing the deposition process of the gas-sensitive film, the light field coupling effect has been enhanced. As a result, the sensor achieves highly sensitive NH3 detection while demonstrating excellent selectivity and stability. The presented room temperature optical fiber NH3 sensor stands as a promising candidate for practical applications in some special narrow spaces and harsh environmental conditions.
Yan-nan Wang: Writing – original draft. Jin Li: Writing – review & editing. Qian Zhang: Data curation. Xihao Gao: Methodology. Zhenyu Yuan: Investigation. Fanli Meng: Supervision.
